target.name: Jerangolid B
target.motivation: Jerangolids are a class of natural products with high antifungal activity and minimal toxicity toward mammals. They present an intriguing synthetic challenge due to their skipped diene substructure with a chiral center between a delta-lactone and a pyran substituent. Their unique mode of action and challenging structural motif make them an interesting synthetic target. Jerangolid B (1) remains elusive despite previous efforts on other jerangolids.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: olefination reactions to generate the skipped diene structure from three respective building blocks
olefination reactions to generate the skipped diene structure from three respective building blocks
olefination reactions to generate the skipped diene structure from three respective building blocks

success_criteria.free_text: First total synthesis of Jerangolid B, confirming the configuration at C14 as R by comparison with published data.

key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection allows for the modular synthesis of the skipped diene core motif from two main building blocks: a chiral allylic trifluoroacetate (lactone fragment 6) and a vinylstannane (pyran fragment 7). The Stille coupling is expected to proceed with inversion of configuration at C8 due to the inner sphere mechanism of hard nucleophiles attacking the pi-allylpalladium complex.
This disconnection allows for the stereoselective formation of the C11 stereocenter in the pyran fragment from aldehyde 10 and TMS acetylene, followed by 6-endo-tet cyclization.
This disconnection allows for the introduction of the C14 methyl group with the correct stereochemistry, starting from ketone 25.
This disconnection allows for the stereoselective formation of the lactone core 17 from aldehyde 14 and silyl ketene acetal 15.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy is modular, aiming to synthesize two complex fragments (lactone 6 and pyran 7) and then join them late in the synthesis via a key sp3-sp2 Stille coupling. This modularity is intended to allow for easy generation of derivatives through modification of either building block. The stereochemistry at C8 is controlled by the Stille coupling, which is expected to proceed with inversion. The stereochemistry at C11 is set by Carreira alkynylation, and at C14 by a stereoselective methylation.

thought.background: Methyl L-(-)-lactate (9) is a commercially available starting material.
thought.gap: The hydroxyl group of methyl L-(-)-lactate needs to be protected to proceed with further transformations.
thought.decision: To protect the hydroxyl group, chose TBSCl, ImH, DMAP in THF, expecting TBS ether 11.

outcome.observation: Methyl L-(-)-lactate (9) was protected as TBS ether 11.

evidence.text_span: Methyl L-(−)-lactate (9) was protected as TBS ether 11
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: TBS ether 11 is available.
thought.gap: The ester needs to be reduced to an aldehyde, and then converted to an unsaturated ester with a specific geometry.
thought.decision: To achieve the unsaturated ester 12, chose DIBALH reduction followed by Wittig olefination, expecting quantitative yield as a 1.5:1 Z/E mixture.

outcome.observation: DIBALH reduction to aldehyde, and subsequent Wittig olefination yielded the unsaturated ester 12 quantitatively as a 1.5:1 Z/E mixture.

evidence.text_span: followed by DIBALH reduction to aldehyde, and subsequent Wittig olefination yielded the unsaturated ester 12 quantitatively as a 1.5:1 Z/E mixture.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Unsaturated ester 12 is available.
thought.gap: The ester needs to be reduced to an allylic alcohol.
thought.decision: To obtain allylic alcohol 13, chose a second DIBALH reduction.

outcome.observation: After a second DIBALH reduction, allylic alcohol 13 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: After a second DIBALH reduction, allylic alcohol 13 was then subjected to the one-pot oxidation/isomerization protocol, which afforded aldehyde 14 in quantitative yields.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Allylic alcohol 13 is available.
thought.gap: Allylic alcohol 13 needs to be converted to aldehyde 14.
thought.decision: To obtain aldehyde 14, chose a one-pot oxidation/isomerization protocol.

outcome.observation: Allylic alcohol 13 was then subjected to the one-pot oxidation/isomerization protocol, which afforded aldehyde 14 in quantitative yields.

evidence.text_span: After a second DIBALH reduction, allylic alcohol 13 was then subjected to the one-pot oxidation/isomerization protocol, which afforded aldehyde 14 in quantitative yields.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Aldehyde 14 and silyl ketene acetal 15 are available. The goal is to perform a vinylogous Mukaiyama aldol reaction (VMAR) to form allylic alcohol 16 with the correct stereochemistry.
thought.gap: Finding conditions for the VMAR that provide high yield and diastereoselectivity for the desired isomer.
thought.decision: To achieve the VMAR, chose (S)-BINOL as the chiral ligand with Ti(OiPr)4 at 20 °C for 4 h, expecting good yields and diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Produced allylic alcohol 16 in good yields (80%) with a 5:95 dr, but Mosher ester analysis revealed it was the undesired diastereomer.

evidence.text_span: We were initially delighted to find that using (S)-BINOL as the chiral ligand (Table 1, entry 1) produced allylic alcohol 16 in good yields with a 5:95 dr.11 However, through Mosher ester analysis, we discovered that the product was the undesired diastereomer.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: The previous attempt with (S)-BINOL yielded the undesired diastereomer. (S)-14 and (S)-BINOL form a matched pair, while (S)-14 and (R)-BINOL form a mismatched pair.
thought.gap: Achieving the desired diastereomer with acceptable yield and dr.
thought.decision: To obtain the desired isomer, simply changed to (R)-BINOL, expecting the desired isomer, albeit potentially with lower yields and dr.

outcome.observation: Changing to (R)-BINOL provided the desired isomer, albeit in lower yields (60%) and dr (83:17).

evidence.text_span: Simply changing to (R)-BINOL provided the desired isomer, albeit in lower yields and dr (Table 1, entry 2).
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: The previous attempt with (R)-BINOL gave the desired isomer but with lower selectivity and yield, likely due to a mismatched pair.
thought.gap: Increasing the selectivity in the mismatched pair.
thought.decision: To increase selectivity, lowered the reaction temperature to -60 °C, expecting improved dr.

outcome.observation: Lowering the reaction temperature to -60 °C yielded 79% of compound 16 with an acceptable dr of 90:10.

evidence.text_span: To increase the selectivity in the mismatched pair, we lowered the reaction temperature to −60 °C (Table 1, entry 3) and obtained 79% compound 16 with an acceptable dr of 90:10.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Previous attempt at -60 °C gave acceptable results. Further optimization of temperature might improve selectivity.
thought.gap: Determining the optimal temperature for the VMAR.
thought.decision: To further increase selectivity, decreased the temperature to -78 °C, expecting better dr.

outcome.observation: Further decreases in the temperature proved to be detrimental, resulting in lower yield (55%) and slightly worse dr (87:13).

evidence.text_span: Further decreases in the temperature proved to be detrimental (Table 1, entry 4).
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Previous VMAR conditions were optimized for temperature. Other catalytic systems might offer better results.
thought.gap: Exploring alternative conditions for the VMAR.
thought.decision: To improve the VMAR, tested conditions described by Carreira et al. using Cu(OTf)2, S-(Tol-BINAP), and PH3SiF2(Bu4N) at -78 °C for 24 h.

outcome.observation: Other conditions described by Carreira et al. were tested but were unsuccessful, yielding 59% with a poor dr of 51:49.

evidence.text_span: Additionally, other conditions described by Carreira et al. were tested (Table 1, entry 5) but were unsuccessful.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Allylic alcohol 16 is available with acceptable dr.
thought.gap: The allylic alcohol needs to be cyclized to form the lactone 17 and then O-methylated.
thought.decision: To obtain lactone 17, chose cyclization under mildly basic conditions with K2CO3, followed by O-methylation using Me2SO4, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Lactone 17 was obtained by cyclization under mildly basic conditions with subsequent O-methylation using Me2SO4 in a good yield (70%).

evidence.text_span: Lactone 17 was obtained by cyclization under mildly basic conditions with subsequent O-methylation using Me2SO4 in a good yield.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Lactone 17 is available. The goal is to O-methylate it.
thought.gap: Finding an alternative O-methylation reagent.
thought.decision: To O-methylate lactone 17, chose TMSCHN2 as an alternative reagent.

outcome.observation: The use of TMSCHN2 also worked as a methylating reagent, albeit in only a 45% yield.

evidence.text_span: T he use of TMSCHN2 also worked as a methylating reagent, albeit in only a 45% yield.
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Lactone 17 is available. The lactone subunit is vulnerable to Lewis acid and basic conditions.
thought.gap: Deprotecting the TBS group and trifluoroacylating the hydroxyl group to form the desired lactone building block 6, while preserving the lactone.
thought.decision: To obtain lactone 6, chose careful deprotection using catalytic amounts of Bi(OTf)3 in water/acetonitrile, followed by trifluoroacylation, expecting good yield over two steps.

outcome.observation: Careful deprotection of compound 17 using catalytical amounts of Bi(OTf)3 in water/acetonitrile, followed by trifluoroacylation, finally led to the desired lactone 6 in 84% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Careful deprotection (vide supra) of compound 17 using catalytical amounts of Bi(OTf)3 in water/acetonitrile,16 followed by trifluoroacylation, finally led to the desired lactone 6 in 84% yield over two steps (Scheme 2).
evidence.section: Synthesis of the lactone building block (Scheme 2)

thought.background: Alkene 18 was prepared in two steps from commercially available 5-trimethylsilyl-pent-4-yn-1-ol.
thought.gap: Alkene 18 needs to be transformed into epoxide 20.
thought.decision: To obtain epoxide 20, chose Shi epoxidation using catalyst 19 derived from L-sorbose, expecting good yield and ee.

outcome.observation: Alkene 18 was then cleanly transformed into epoxide 20 through Shi epoxidation, using catalyst 19 derived from L-sorbose, with 75% yield and 92% ee.

evidence.text_span: Alkene 18 was then cleanly transformed into epoxide 20 through Shi epoxidation, using catalyst 19 derived from L-sorbose, with 75% yield and 92% ee.

thought.background: Epoxide 20 is available.
thought.gap: Epoxide 20 needs to be oxidized to aldehyde 10.
thought.decision: To obtain aldehyde 10, chose DMP oxidation, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Subsequent DMP oxidation delivered aldehyde 10 in 83% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent DMP oxidation delivered aldehyde 10 in 83% yield.

thought.background: Aldehyde 10 is available but decomposes quickly. The goal is to generate the stereocenter at C11 via Carreira alkynylation.
thought.gap: Performing Carreira alkynylation without decomposition of the starting material.
thought.decision: To achieve Carreira alkynylation, used standard Carreira conditions, expecting propargyl alcohol 21.

outcome.observation: Our initial runs using standard Carreira conditions led to complete decomposition of the starting material. Compound 10 is an aliphatic aldehyde, which can easily react with itself via aldol addition under these conditions.

evidence.text_span: Our initial runs using standard Carreira conditions led to complete decomposition of the starting material. Compound 10 is an aliphatic aldehyde, which can easily react with itself via aldol addition under these conditions.

thought.background: Standard Carreira conditions led to decomposition of aldehyde 10 due to self-reaction. Aldehyde 10 needs to be added slowly, and TMS acetylene needs to be in excess.
thought.gap: Successfully performing Carreira alkynylation to obtain propargyl alcohol 21.
thought.decision: To prevent decomposition and achieve the reaction, added aldehyde 10 very slowly via a syringe pump, used TMS acetylene in great excess, and optimized conditions to 1.1 equiv of Zn(OTf)2, 1.2 equiv of NEt3, and 1.2 equiv of (+)-NME at 60 °C, adding aldehyde 10 over 10 h.

outcome.observation: Our best results were achieved by using 1.1 equiv of Zn(OTf)2, 1.2 equiv of NEt3, and 1.2 equiv of (+)-NME at 60 °C and adding aldehyde 10 over 10 h, obtaining propagyl alcohol 21, which was directly cyclized without purification.

evidence.text_span: Our best results were achieved by using 1.1 equiv of Zn(OTf)2, 1.2 equiv of NEt3, and 1.2 equiv of (+)-NME at 60 °C and adding aldehyde 10 over 10 h, obtaining propagyl alcohol 21, which was directly cyclized using BF3·OEt2 without purification.

thought.background: Propargyl alcohol 21 was obtained and used directly without purification.
thought.gap: Propargyl alcohol 21 needs to be cyclized to form the 6-endo-tet product 22.
thought.decision: To achieve 6-endo-tet cyclization, used BF3·OEt2, expecting compound 22 as a 10:1 mixture of separable diastereomers, facilitated by the TMS group.

outcome.observation: This led to 6-endo-tet cyclization, forming compound 22 as a 10:1 mixture of separable diastereomers. The TMS group at the axial position was pivotal for the success of this step.

evidence.text_span: This led to 6-endo-tet cyclization, forming compound 22 as a 10:1 mixture of separable diastereomers. Usually, the spiro transition state leading to 5-exo-tet product 23 is favored over the fused transition state leading to the preferred 6-endo-tet product 22 in epoxide openings.20 The installation of a TMS group at the axial position was therefore pivotal for the success of this step, as it facilitates the 6-endo-tet cyclization by selectively weakening the adjacent C−O bond through a favorable σC-Si-2po orbital interaction and stabilizing the developing positive charge in the transition state.21

thought.background: Compound 22 is available. The TMS group at C15 needs to be cleaved to allow for methylation at the Si site.
thought.gap: Cleaving the TMS groups to obtain compound 24.
thought.decision: To cleave the TMS groups, chose protiodesilylation with TBAF, first with TBAF/AcOH for the acetylenic TMS group, then with TBAF for the remaining TMS group, expecting compound 24 in good yield.

outcome.observation: Through protiodesilylation of the TMS groups with TBAF, we obtained compound 24 in an 87% yield. The acetylenic TMS group had to be cleaved first with a mixture of TBAF/AcOH, as just using an excess amount of TBAF sometimes leads to major formation of the Peterson olefination product.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, through protiodesilylation of the TMS groups with TBAF, we obtained compound 24 in an 87% yield. The acetylenic TMS group had to be cleaved first with a mixture of TBAF/AcOH, as just using an excess amount of TBAF sometimes leads to major formation of the Peterson olefination product.

thought.background: Compound 24 is available.
thought.gap: Compound 24 needs to be oxidized to ketone 25.
thought.decision: To obtain ketone 25, chose subsequent DMP oxidation, expecting near-quantitative yields.

outcome.observation: Subsequent oxidation afforded ketone 25 in near-quantitative yields.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent oxidation afforded ketone 25 in near-quantitative yields.

thought.background: Ketone 25 is available. Both terminal acetylene and the alpha positions in ketone 25 are CH-acidic, making stereoselective methylation challenging.
thought.gap: Achieving clean stereoselective methylation without inducing deprotonation and side reactions, aiming for the desired 14-(R) epimer.
thought.decision: To methylate ketone 25, explored the ZnCl2-catalyzed addition of Grignard reagents developed by Hatano et al., using 1.1 equiv of MeMgBr and 0.1 equiv of ZnCl2.

outcome.observation: Attained high yields (70%) in overall methylation, but it unfortunately resulted primarily in the unwanted 14-(S) epimer in a 39:61 fashion.

evidence.text_span: Using MeMgBr and a catalytic amount of ZnCl2, we attained high yields in overall methylation, but it unfortunately resulted primarily in the unwanted 14-(S) epimer in a 39:61 fashion (Table 2, entry 1).

thought.background: Previous attempt with MeMgBr/ZnCl2 yielded the unwanted 14-(S) epimer. Increasing steric demand in the ate complex might favor attack from the Si site.
thought.gap: Improving diastereoselectivity towards the desired 14-(R) epimer.
thought.decision: To increase steric demand, used catalytic amounts of TMSCH2MgCl to introduce the TMSCH2 group as a non-transferable dummy ligand, along with MeMgBr and ZnCl2/LiCl.

outcome.observation: Using catalytic amounts of TMSCH2MgCl neither increased the yield (74%) nor improved the diastereomeric ratio (45:55).

evidence.text_span: However, using catalytic amounts of TMSCH2MgCl to introduce the TMSCH2 group as a non-transferable dummy ligand to increase steric demand of the ate complex neither increased the yield nor improved the diastereomeric ratio (Table 2, entry 2).

thought.background: Previous attempts to improve diastereoselectivity with Grignard reagents were unsuccessful. Stoichiometric amounts of the ate complex might be more effective.
thought.gap: Further improving diastereoselectivity towards the desired 14-(R) epimer.
thought.decision: To improve selectivity, used stoichiometric amounts of (TMSCH2)2ZnMeLi (generated from MeLi and TMSCH2MgCl) with ZnCl2/LiCl.

outcome.observation: Using stochiometric amounts of (TMSCH2)2ZnMeLi had no noticeable effect either, yielding 72% with a 37:63 dr.

evidence.text_span: Using stochiometric amounts of (TMSCH2)2ZnMeLi had no noticeable effect either (Table 2, entry 3).

thought.background: ZnCl2-catalyzed additions were unsuccessful in achieving the desired stereoselectivity. Imamoto's procedure using CeCl3 might offer a different selectivity profile.
thought.gap: Exploring alternative methylation procedures to obtain the desired 14-(R) epimer.
thought.decision: To improve selectivity, changed to Imamoto's procedure using MeMgBr/CeCl3.

outcome.observation: Changing to Imamoto's procedure using MeMgBr/CeCl3 unsurprisingly led to the same results, yielding 76% with a 40:60 dr.

evidence.text_span: Changing to Imamoto's procedure using MeMgBr/CeCl3 unsurprisingly led to the same results (Table 2, entry 4).

thought.background: MeMgBr/CeCl3 did not improve selectivity. MeLi reacts with CeCl3 to form a different organocerium species, which might alter selectivity.
thought.gap: Achieving the desired 14-(R) epimer with improved diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To improve selectivity, used 1.0 equiv of MeLi and 1.0 equiv of CeCl3, expecting a change in selectivity.

outcome.observation: Using 1.0 equiv of MeLi and 1.0 equiv of CeCl3 resulted in the desired epimer 26 in an 83:17 ratio, albeit in a lower overall yield (52%).

evidence.text_span: To our delight, using 1.0 equiv of MeLi and 1.0 equiv of CeCl3 resulted in the desired epimer 26 in an 83:17 ratio, albeit in a lower overall yield (Table 2, entry 5).

thought.background: Using 1.0 equiv of MeLi and 1.0 equiv of CeCl3 gave good dr but lower yield. Adjusting the stoichiometry of MeLi might improve yield while maintaining selectivity.
thought.gap: Maximizing overall yield while maintaining acceptable diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To improve yield, increased MeLi to 2.0 equiv while keeping CeCl3 at 1.0 equiv.

outcome.observation: Increasing MeLi to 2.0 equiv resulted in 69% yield with a 61:39 dr.

evidence.text_span: Using 2.0 equiv of MeLi resulted in 69% yield with a 61:39 dr (Table 2, entry 6).

thought.background: Further increasing MeLi might improve yield. The diastereoselectivity is still acceptable as epimers can be separated.
thought.gap: Maximizing overall yield while maintaining acceptable diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To improve yield, increased MeLi to 3.0 equiv while keeping CeCl3 at 1.0 equiv.

outcome.observation: Increasing MeLi to 3.0 equiv resulted in 75% yield with a 75:25 dr.

evidence.text_span: Using 3.0 equiv of MeLi resulted in 75% yield with a 75:25 dr (Table 2, entry 7).

thought.background: Further increasing MeLi and CeCl3 might maximize yield. The diastereoselectivity is still acceptable as epimers can be separated.
thought.gap: Maximizing overall yield while maintaining acceptable diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To maximize overall yields, used 4.5 equiv of MeLi and 1.5 equiv of CeCl3.

outcome.observation: A mixture of 4.5 equiv of MeLi and 1.5 equiv of CeCl3 maximized overall yields to 84% while still delivering an acceptable diastereomeric ratio of 68:32. Both epimers could be separated by flash chromatography.

evidence.text_span: Overall, we found that a mixture of 4.5 equiv of MeLi and 1.5 equiv of CeCl3 maximized overall yields to 84% while still delivering an acceptable diastereomeric ratio of 68:32 (Table 2, entry 8). As both epimers could be separated by flash chromatography, no further optimizations were conducted.

thought.background: The methylation conditions were optimized for yield and dr. Chiral ligands might further improve diastereoselectivity.
thought.gap: Improving diastereoselectivity using a chiral ligand.
thought.decision: To improve dr, used 1.5 equiv of (R)-BINOL with 4.5 equiv of MeLi and 1.5 equiv of CeCl3.

outcome.observation: Using 1.5 equiv of (R)-BINOL resulted in a lower yield (30%) despite a slightly better dr (86:14).

evidence.text_span: A total of 1.5 equiv of (R)-BINOL was used.

thought.background: Tertiary alcohol 26 is available.
thought.gap: The tertiary alcohol needs to be protected as a TES ether.
thought.decision: To protect tertiary alcohol 26, chose TES protection using TESOTf, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: TES protection of tertiary alcohol 26 using TESOTf afforded compound 27 in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: TES protection of tertiary alcohol 26 using TESOTf afforded compound 27 in 91% yield.

thought.background: Compound 27 is available.
thought.gap: Compound 27 needs to be converted to the desired vinylstannane 7.
thought.decision: To obtain vinylstannane 7, chose stannylcupration of alkyne 27 with subsequent trapping of vinylcuprate with MeI, expecting near-quantitative yields after reversed-phase chromatography.

outcome.observation: With stannylcupration of alkyne 27 with subsequent trapping of vinylcuprate with MeI, we obtained the desired vinylstannane 7 in near-quantitative yields (90%) after reversed-phase chromatography.

evidence.text_span: With stannylcupration of alkyne 27 with subsequent trapping of vinylcuprate with MeI,29 we obtained the desired vinylstannane 7 in near-quantitative yields after reversed-phase chromatography.

thought.background: Lactone building block 6 and vinylstannane building block 7 are available. The key step is the sp3-sp2 Stille coupling.
thought.gap: Performing the Stille coupling to generate the skipped diene structure, expecting inversion of configuration at C8.
thought.decision: To achieve the coupling, utilized Pd2dba3 as a catalyst, along with molecular sieves 4Å under microwave irradiation in a 2:1 mixture of THF and DMF, expecting coupling product 28 and branched isomer 29.

outcome.observation: The best yields for the coupling of compound 6 with compound 7 were achieved by utilizing Pd2dba3 as a catalyst, along with molecular sieves 4Å under microwave irradiation in a 2:1 mixture of THF and DMF. After 10 cycles of pulsed microwave irradiation, we obtained 50% of the coupling product as a 2:1 linear/branched (28/29) mixture, which afforded 33% of pure compound 28 after chromatographic purification. 13C NMR indicated no epimerization at the central carbon center at C8.

evidence.text_span: Through extensive experimentation, we found that the best yields for the coupling of compound 6 with compound 7 were achieved by utilizing Pd2dba3 as a catalyst, along with molecular sieves 4Å (to ensure anhydrous conditions) under microwave irradiation in a 2:1 mixture of THF and DMF. After 10 cycles of pulsed microwave irradiation (50 W, 1 min of irradiation, and 1 min of cooldown), we obtained 50% of the coupling product as a 2:1 linear/branched (1/b) mixture, which afforded 33% of pure compound 28 after chromatographic purification. 13C NMR indicated no epimerization at the central carbon center at C8.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Final Steps in the Synthesis of Jerangolid B 1

thought.background: Coupling product 28 is available. The TES group needs to be removed to yield jerangolid B (1). The TBS ether could not be cleaved under standard conditions without major decomposition of the lactone unit.
thought.gap: Removing the TES group under mild conditions that preserve the lactone unit.
thought.decision: To deprotect the TES group, chose 5 mol % Bi(OTf)3 in H2O/MeCN, expecting quantitative yield of jerangolid B (1).

outcome.observation: Removal of the TES group under mild conditions using 5 mol % Bi(OTf)3 ultimately afforded jerangolid B (1) in quantitative yields. The use of TES was deliberate, as the TBS ether could not be cleaved under standard conditions without major decomposition of the lactone unit in compound 1.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the TES group under mild conditions using 5 mol % Bi(OTf)3 ultimately afforded jerangolid B (1) in quantitative yields. The use of TES was deliberate, as the TBS ether could not be cleaved under standard conditions without major decomposition of the lactone unit in compound 1.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Final Steps in the Synthesis of Jerangolid B 1

thought.background: Jerangolid B (1) has been synthesized.
thought.gap: Confirming the configuration at C14.
thought.decision: To confirm the configuration, compared the NMR data of synthetic jerangolid B (1) to the data of 14-epi-jerangolid B and natural jerangolid B.

outcome.observation: By comparison of the NMR data of 14-epi-jerangolid B to the data of compound 1 and natural jerangolid B, we could show that C14 has a R configuration.

evidence.text_span: By comparison of the NMR data of 14-epi-jerangolid B (1)22 to the data of compound 1 and natural jerangolid B, we could show that C14 has a R configuration.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Final Steps in the Synthesis of Jerangolid B 1

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the total synthesis.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Number of steps in the longest linear sequence.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of steps in the synthesis.

validation.comparison_to_prior: This is the first total synthesis of jerangolid B. The synthesis confirmed the R configuration at C14 by comparison of NMR data with 14-epi-jerangolid B and natural jerangolid B.
validation.significance: This work reports the first total synthesis of jerangolid B, a natural product with high antifungal activity. The modular approach, incorporating an sp3-sp2 Stille coupling as the key step, provides a general method for accessing jerangolids and their derivatives. The synthesis also definitively established the C14 configuration as R, resolving a previous ambiguity.

source.title: Total Synthesis of Jerangolid B via sp<sup>3</sup>-sp<sup>2</sup> Stille Coupling.
source.authors: Schug J, Morgenstern B, Jauch J.
